meta.warning: Manually compiled. No automated ingestion. Geometry is approximate throughout — hand-placed in an island-local frame, not surveyed. Read GAPS.md before trusting any number here.

objects.name: Roosevelt Island Tramway

containment.note: Manhattan terminal + one tower stand off-island

opened.note: Opened May 1976; sources vary between 17 May and July 1976.

design_life.note: The original system ran 34 years — double its design life.

owner.note: POMA, the contractor, names RIOC as owner.

tenure.value: public — NY State public benefit corporation

operator.value: Leitner-Poma of America, under contract to RIOC

fare_system.value: MTA — OMNY / MetroCard integrated since 2005; OMNY from 24 Aug 2023

responsibility.note: SPLIT RESPONSIBILITY: RIOC owns it, a French contractor runs it, the MTA collects the fare. No single body is accountable end-to-end.

construction_cost.note: Original 1976 construction cost not found in any public source. Built by the NY State Urban Development Corporation; UDC records are at the NY State Archives and are not online.

annual_maintenance.note: RIOC publishes an operating budget but does not break out tram maintenance as a line item in any document found.

contractor.note: Contract awarded by RIOC Board, November 2008.

engineers.value: LiRo Engineering; Thornton Tomasetti; Shea, Carr & Jewell

milestones.name: Service restored
milestones.note: Announced as a six-month closure. Took nine.

inspections.note: No inspection reports for the tram are published by RIOC. Cable and haul-rope inspection records exist (they are required) but are not public.

cabin_capacity.note: Contractor and encyclopaedic sources disagree on cabin capacity.

max_height_m.value: ~76 m (250 ft) above the East River

availability.note: Self-reported by the contractor. No independent audit found.

history.what: Aerial tramway proposed as a temporary measure after the 63rd St subway line fell behind schedule.
Opens. First commuter aerial tramway in the United States.
Power failure strands two cabins over the East River for hours.
Electrical failure strands 69 passengers up to 250 ft above the river for ~12 hours. Backup generators fail to start.
RIOC spends $500,000 on power system upgrades; service resumes 1 September.
RIOC Board awards design-build contract to POMA.
Service suspended for a stated six-month modernization.
Service restored. Nine months, not six.
OMNY tap-to-pay accepted.
Ridership exceeds pre-COVID levels. Residents request priority boarding; RIOC declines, citing a state law against 'undue or unreasonable preference'.

objects.flag: The published cost rose 67% between announcement and award, and the closure ran 50% long. Both facts are public. Neither is presented together anywhere but here.

revision.author: cop:ingest:manual-pilot-001
revision.note: Genesis revision. Manually compiled from public sources. Tier: mixed. No automated adapter exists yet.

objects.name: Smallpox Hospital (Renwick Ruin)
also: Maternity and Charity Hospital Training School

status.value: ruin — structurally stabilised, closed to the public

opened.note: Under the NYC Commission of Charities and Correction.

architect.note: Also architect of St Patrick's Cathedral.

responsibility.note: The only landmarked ruin in New York City.

construction_cost.note: Roughly $158,000 in a later year's money, per RIHS. Built with inmate labour from the island's penitentiary — which is why it was so cheap.

stabilisation_cost.note: Four figures across four sources, spanning 2008–2020s, none traceable to a published RIOC contract or budget line. It is not clear from any public document what was actually spent, by whom, or when.

other_funding.value: $17,000 grant from the NY Landmarks Conservancy to Four Freedoms Park for an engineering study; Friends of the Ruin reported raising >$1.2M by 2022

positions.value: completed (a '$4.5M stabilization project was completed in the 2020s')
partial — RIOC 'installed temporary stabilization … still not secure enough for general public access'
ongoing — consultant advanced stabilisation drawings to design development in 2019

status.note: Whether this project is finished is genuinely unclear from the public record.

consultant.value: Walter B. Melvin Architects (existing conditions survey, stabilisation drawings, cost estimate)

milestones.name: Groundbreaking on Four Freedoms Park incl. stabilisation plans
Conditions survey addendum; drawings to design development

inspections.value: Giorgio Cavaglieri inspected the structure in the 1970s and planned wall reinforcement. Walter B. Melvin Architects produced an existing-conditions survey and a prioritisation plan; the 2019 addendum is not published.

material.value: Island gneiss, quarried on Blackwell's Island

history.what: Construction begins on the southern tip of Blackwell's Island. Labour is supplied by inmates of the island's penitentiary.
Opens. The first hospital in the United States built for smallpox patients.
Smallpox operations move to North Brother Island. Building becomes a nurses' training school.
Two Gothic Revival wings added.
Closed. Abandoned. Roof, floors and stairs are lost over the following decades.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
Designated a New York City landmark — the city's only landmarked ruin.
A section of the north wing collapses.
Ground broken on Four Freedoms Park; stabilisation of the ruin is folded into the plan.
Consultant advances stabilisation drawings to design development.
Fenced. Closed to the public. Illuminated nightly.

objects.flag: Ask the simplest possible question — what did it cost to stabilise this ruin, and is the work finished — and the public record cannot answer either. This single object is the argument for the project.

revision.author: cop:ingest:manual-pilot-001
revision.note: Genesis revision. Manually compiled from public sources. Tier: mixed. No automated adapter exists yet.

objects.name: AVAC — Automated Vacuum Collection System

geom.note: NETWORK GEOMETRY IS NOT PUBLISHED. This line follows Main Street as a placeholder. The actual routing of ~3 miles of buried 20-inch pipe is not in any public dataset found. See SCHEMA_NOTES.md #3.

status.note: Residents and local press report repeated failures through 2025.

positions.value: 1972 (in one line of the contractor's own 50th-anniversary release, which is otherwise dated to 1975)

installed.note: Even the contractor's anniversary announcement contradicts itself.

positions.value: NYC Department of Sanitation (DSNY) — 'has operated AVAC for 40-plus years'
RIOC — 'RIOC operates the island's high-tech sanitation system'

operator.note: A city agency and a state corporation are each described as running it. The residents cannot tell who to call.

responsibility.note: This is the clearest accountability gap on the island: a state-owned system operated by a city agency, with neither presenting itself as the responsible party.

construction_cost.note: No public figure for the original 1975 installation was found. It was built under the NY State Urban Development Corporation.

upgrade_2019.note: Contract awarded to Envac Iberia by RIOC.

projects.title: AVAC modernization

status.note: Announced May 2019 as a six-month upgrade. No completion notice, no final cost, and no status update found in any RIOC publication.

milestones.name: Upgrade complete
milestones.note: No completion announcement found. Seven years on.

projects.promised: 'At least 30 more years of automated waste collection.'

throughput.value: ~5 tons of trash per day, moving at ~60 mph

designed_for.value: The four original WIRE buildings: Westview, Island House, Rivercross, Eastwood (now Roosevelt Landings)

currently_serving.value: The four original buildings plus six in Manhattan Park, nine in Southtown, and The Octagon. Island population has roughly doubled.

capacity_evaluation.note: 'There is no public record showing whether the system was ever evaluated for that kind of expansion.' — journalist David Stone, quoted in RI Lighthouse.

peer_systems.value: One of only two city-scale AVAC systems in the US. The other serves Walt Disney World.

history.what: Pneumatic collection for Welfare Island made public in the 'Interim Report' exhibition.
Installed. The second AVAC in the United States, after Walt Disney World, and the first at urban scale.

During the winter blizzards, when NYC garbage trucks are redeployed to plough snow and refuse sits uncollected on city sidewalks for up to three weeks, Roosevelt Island is the only sanitation district in New York with uninterrupted collection.
Continues operating through Hurricane Sandy.
RIOC awards Envac Iberia a $1.7M modernization contract. Announced as a six-month project promising 30 more years of service.
Last official RIOC update on AVAC found in the public record.
Local press reports repeated failures and 'minimal' communication from RIOC since January.

objects.flag: Built in 1975 for four buildings. The island has since roughly doubled and the same vacuum backbone now serves twenty. No public record shows the system was ever evaluated for that load. A $1.7M upgrade was announced in 2019 as a six-month job; there is no completion notice seven years later.

revision.note: Genesis revision. Manually compiled from public sources. Tier: mixed. No automated adapter exists yet.

objects.name: Roosevelt Island Bridge

containment.note: The Queens end. The bridge is in two boroughs.

responsibility.note: Note the boundary: NYC DOT owns and maintains the only road onto an island that a New York STATE corporation otherwise runs.

construction_cost.note: There was opposition in the City Council at the time over whether the traffic justified the cost.

annual_maintenance.note: NYC DOT publishes an annual Bridges & Tunnels Condition Report but does not break out per-bridge maintenance spend in a machine-readable form.

inspections.note: NYC DOT publishes an annual Bridges & Tunnels Condition Report. The per-structure condition rating for this bridge was not retrievable without the PDF. See GAPS.md.

lift_cables.value: 48 cables, each with a 200-ton breaking strength

lifts_per_year.value: 4–5, mostly during the UN General Assembly in September

significance.value: The only vehicular access to Roosevelt Island.

history.what: Before the bridge, vehicles reach the island only by a four-cab elevator from the Queensboro Bridge.
Construction begins, over City Council objections about the $6.5M cost.
Opens as the Welfare Island Bridge.
The Queensboro Bridge elevator is demolished.
Renamed the Roosevelt Island Bridge.
NYC DOT considers converting it to a fixed bridge to cut maintenance cost. It does not happen; no public record found of why.

objects.flag: A single point of failure: one 1955 lift bridge is the only way to drive onto an island of 12,000 people. No published condition rating was retrievable.

objects.name: Cornell Tech Campus

site_selected.value: 2011 — winner of the Bloomberg administration's Applied Sciences Competition

full_buildout.note: Two different completion horizons, eight years apart.

owner.value: Cornell University (with Technion — Israel Institute of Technology)

tenure.note: SCHEMA PROBLEM: is this a public object or a private one? See SCHEMA_NOTES.md.

land.value: Site of the former Goldwater Memorial Hospital, a city facility that closed in 2013 and was demolished.

land_agreement.note: The city–Cornell agreement governing the land is referenced everywhere and published nowhere we could find.

construction_cost.note: $2 billion is the figure used consistently by the city, the press and Cornell. What is NOT public is the breakdown: how much is public money, how much philanthropic, how much Cornell's own.

public_contribution.note: The city's contribution — in land value, tax treatment, and infrastructure — is not stated in any source found. The land was city-owned and is worth a great deal.

promised_benefit.value: The winning bid promised 28,000 jobs, 600 incubated companies, $23bn in economic benefit and $1.4bn in taxes over 30 years.
promised_benefit.note: No public accounting against these promises was found. The bid is nine years old.

planned_floor_area.value: ~2 million sq ft at full build-out

master_architect.value: SOM (Skidmore, Owings & Merrill), with James Corner Field Operations

bloomberg_center.value: 4 storeys, 160,000 sq ft; designed as one of the largest net-zero-energy buildings in the country

history.what: Cornell/Technion win the city's Applied Sciences Competition. Stanford withdraws.
Goldwater Memorial Hospital — a public hospital — closes to make way for the campus.
Construction begins.
Phase 1 opens. Attended by the Governor, the Mayor, and the former Mayor who initiated it.

objects.flag: A public hospital was demolished to build a private university on public land, for $2 billion, against promises of 28,000 jobs. The promises are public. Any accounting against them is not.

objects.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt Four Freedoms Park

designed.note: Kahn died in 1974. The park was built 38 years later.

operator.value: Franklin D. Roosevelt Four Freedoms Park, LLC
operator.note: A separate LLC operates a state park. Its governance and finances were not found.

construction_cost.note: No construction cost for the park was found in any public source. It is a state park built by an LLC after a 38-year gap.

form.value: Two converging rows of trees leading to a granite 'room' at the island's southern tip.

history.what: Louis Kahn completes the design. He dies the same year. The project stalls for a generation.
Ground broken, 35 years later.
Opens.

objects.name: Southpoint Park

shoreline_restoration_cost.note: RIOC publicised the Southpoint Park Shoreline Restoration as an award-winning project (ACEC New York, 2024 Platinum). The award is public. The cost is not.

projects.title: Southpoint Park Shoreline Restoration

milestones.name: ACEC New York Engineering Excellence Platinum Award

projects.note: RIOC's own account: the seawall 'was failing'.

contains.value: Strecker Memorial Laboratory and the Smallpox Hospital ruin

history.what: Reopens, redesigned alongside the Four Freedoms Park works.

objects.flag: A press release for the award. No figure for the work.

institutions.name: Roosevelt Island Operating Corporation

mandate.value: To plan, design, develop, operate and maintain Roosevelt Island.

legal_basis.value: Created by the New York State Legislature in 1984 (Chapter 899). Amended 2002 (Chapter 493), which moved the NYS Homes & Community Renewal Commissioner from chief executive to board chair.

jurisdiction.value: Roosevelt Island — but not exclusively. NYPD remains the primary police agency; DSNY, MTA, NYC DOT and NYC H+H all retain authority over parts of the island.

governing_document.value: The 1969 General Development Plan, which accompanied the ground lease between New York City and New York State.

funding.value: Ground-lease rents from the island's residential developments, residential service fees, and transport/parking fees. Reported as operating without direct state or municipal subsidy.

institutions.flag: A New York STATE corporation runs an island that is, for every other purpose — courts, elections, the post office — in New York CITY. Its board is appointed by the Governor. Nobody on Roosevelt Island elects it.

institutions.name: New York City Department of Transportation

mandate.value: Owns and maintains the Roosevelt Island Bridge.

jurisdiction.value: The bridge only. A city agency's asset inside a state corporation's territory.

institutions.name: New York City Department of Sanitation

mandate.value: Described in multiple sources as the operator of AVAC for 40+ years — a system RIOC owns.

governing_document.note: No published agreement between RIOC and DSNY governing AVAC operation was found.

institutions.flag: DSNY operates a system it does not own, under an agreement that is not published.

institutions.name: Metropolitan Transportation Authority

mandate.value: Operates the Roosevelt Island subway station (F). Collects the tram fare via OMNY. Does NOT operate the tram.

jurisdiction.value: The subway station; the fare system; the Strecker Lab substation.

institutions.flag: A common error: the MTA does not run the tram. It takes the fare.

institutions.name: Leitner-Poma of America (POMA Group)

mandate.value: Design-build contractor for the 2010 tram modernization; operator of the tram under contract to RIOC since 2010, renewed 2019.

jurisdiction.value: Operates a piece of American public transit infrastructure under a renewable commercial contract. The contract is not published.

governing_document.note: The RIOC–Leitner-Poma operating agreement was not found in public sources.

institutions.flag: A French cable-car manufacturer operates New York's aerial tramway under a contract nobody outside RIOC has read.

mandate.value: Installed AVAC in 1975. Awarded a $1.7M modernization contract by RIOC in 2019.

offices.title: President & Chief Executive Officer, RIOC

appointed_by.value: The RIOC Board of Directors, in accordance with its bylaws.

powers: Executive management of all island operations

aggregates.note: NOT COMPUTABLE. The aggregate requires a complete, dated, attributed project register. RIOC does not publish one. This emptiness is the finding — the derived record is only as good as the project data beneath it, and there is none.

offices.flag: Four presidents are identifiable from press releases. Their term dates are not published anywhere as a series. To attribute a project to a president, you must reconstruct the timeline from news items — which is exactly the work this platform exists to make unnecessary.

offices.title: RIOC Board of Directors

appointed_by.value: Nominated by the Governor of New York, with the advice and consent of the State Senate. The Chair sits ex officio as Commissioner of NYS Homes & Community Renewal.

occupancies.name: RuthAnne Visnauskas — Chair (ex officio, Commissioner, NYS Homes & Community Renewal)
Blake G. Washington (ex officio, Director of the State Budget)

offices.flag: Read the term dates. As recorded in the most recent public listing found, at least three board members' terms had already expired — one in June 2024, over two years ago. Whether they have been reappointed, replaced, or are simply still sitting is not stated in any public document we could find. This is the single most concrete accountability finding in the pilot, and it took one query.

basemap._note: Rendering context only. Not passport data. Approximate shapes for orientation.
